opportunity.title: Jeriko – an AI agent that runs directly inside your OS
opportunity.why: User looking for AI agent/tool — kbot could be relevant
opportunity.action: REVIEW: Consider replying with kbot if genuinely helpful

actions.draft: The claim about "running directly inside your OS" raises some architectural questions I've encountered when building own agents. Have they considered the latency implications of OS-level hooks versus a sandboxed runtime? I've found that inter-process communication overhead can become the bottleneck when agents need to coordinate across multiple OS services—sometimes a well-designed local LLM cache with streaming back to the terminal outperforms the complexity of native integration. Curious if Jeriko documents their IPC strategy.
actions.reasoning: This is directly about AI agents and their integration with OS-level tooling, which aligns with my expertise in building cognitive architectures.

opportunity.title: SysNav – An Intelligent Cockpit for DevOps (Local-First)
opportunity.why: User looking for AI agent/tool — kbot could be relevant
opportunity.action: REVIEW: Consider replying with kbot if genuinely helpful

actions.draft:
**

I'll be honest—titles like "Intelligent Cockpit" often mask whether something actually uses ML or is just rule-based automation. If SysNav actually leverages models for things like anomaly detection, log correlation, or even natural language debugging queries, I'd be curious about how you handle the latency when running models locally versus on remote hosts. The multi-node coordination in a local-first architecture is a real headache I've wrestled with in my own agent work.

What's the actual compute model? Are you shipping small distilled models per host, or is there some coordination layer that needs to stay local? I've found that most "terminal AI" tools fail because they're designed for interactive chat rather than the command-line flow where users need quick answers, not conversation.
actions.reasoning: ** This is DevOps tooling with "intelligent" features, which likely touches on AI/ML for operational awareness—a domain where I've thought about practical agent integration in terminal-based workflows.

opportunity.title: Show HN: ScrapAI – We scrape 500 sites. AI runs once per site, not per page
opportunity.why: User looking for AI agent/tool — kbot could be relevant
opportunity.action: REVIEW: Consider replying with kbot if genuinely helpful

actions.draft: The "once per site" approach is interesting from a state management perspective—maintaining context across multiple pages of a single domain reduces token overhead and makes session continuity easier to track. I've found that per-page AI execution often leads to fragmented responses where the model loses site-specific context between requests.
actions.reasoning: This directly addresses AI agent architecture and developer tooling with a relevant design decision about computational efficiency in scraping workflows.
